title: The generated yaml file does not work the same way as if I manually created a pod
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

When I manually create a pod then attach containers everything works. But when i try to play a generated kube file i got a permission denied errors.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

1. I manually create a pod and attach containers:
``` sh
podman pod create --name=droplet -p 8080:80 -p 8443:443

podman run -d --name nginx --pod droplet \
    -v /var/pods/droplet/nginx:/etc/nginx:ro,Z \
    -v ~/.acme.sh/example.com:/etc/letsencrypt:ro,Z \
     nginx:1.19.1-alpine

podman run -d --name n8n --pod droplet \
    -v /var/pods/droplet/n8n:/root/.n8n:Z \
    -e GENERIC_TIMEZONE="Europe/Moscow" \
    -e N8N_BASIC_AUTH_ACTIVE=true \
    -e N8N_BASIC_AUTH_USER="user" \
    -e N8N_BASIC_AUTH_PASSWORD="password" \
    -e N8N_HOST="example.comi" \
    -e VUE_APP_URL_BASE_API="example.comi" \
    -e N8N_ENCRYPTION_KEY="secret-token-key" \
    -e NODE_FUNCTION_ALLOW_BUILTIN="*" \
    -e NODE_FUNCTION_ALLOW_EXTERNAL="lodash" \
    -e WEBHOOK_TUNNEL_URL="https://example.com" \
     n8nio/n8n:0.76.0
```
Everything works here.

2. So, lets generate a `droplet.yml` by `podman generate kube droplet > droplet.yml` command.
3. `podman play kube droplet.yml`

**Describe the results you received:**
My containers exited with errors codes because `permission denied` to all volumes.

**Describe the results you expected:**
Everything works like it works with just commands from step 1.


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
```
Version:      2.0.4
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.14.6
Built:        Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
APIVersion: 1
Built: 0
BuiltTime: Thu Jan  1 00:00:00 1970
GitCommit: ""
GoVersion: go1.14.6
OsArch: linux/amd64
Version: 2.0.4
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
podman-2.0.4-1.fc32.x86_64
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Rootless mode.

comments.body:
Hello, to the best of my knowledge, Kubernetes does not have an equivalent for podman's `:Z` flag (which relabels the volume to match a unique-per-container selinux label). The Kubernetes selinux support is to specify [a fixed label](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/security-context/#assign-selinux-labels-to-a-container) for the container, which is used for all volumes.

So it makes sense that running the pod through kube YAML lost the `:Z` volume option. A workaround would be using `:z` instead, which relabels the volume for any container -- then running the pod through kube yaml should work in the future since the host directory is already labelled correctly.

This doesn't solve your problem, just providing some insight from how Kubernetes handles selinux (which is, not well). One possibility to support Z and z flags in the kube yaml would be storing those flags in a kube annotation [similar to this](https://github.com/stardustapp/cloud-infra/blob/master/apps/linuxserver-base/statefulset.yaml#L17).

Hi! Thank you for answering.

Unfortunately, I use the *.yml file with the podman cli. The documentation for `podman play kube` says: 
`Note: HostPath volume types created by play kube will be given an SELinux private label (Z)`

http://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-play-kube.1.html.

So I expect the generated podman file to work as if I had manually created a pod and added containers to it.

> Hello, to the best of my knowledge, Kubernetes does not have an equivalent for podman's `:Z` flag (which relabels the volume to match a unique-per-container selinux label). The Kubernetes selinux support is to specify [a fixed label](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/security-context/#assign-selinux-labels-to-a-container) for the container, which is used for all volumes.
> 
> So it makes sense that running the pod through kube YAML lost the `:Z` volume option. A workaround would be using `:z` instead, which relabels the volume for any container -- then running the pod through kube yaml should work in the future since the host directory is already labelled correctly.
> 
> This doesn't solve your problem, just providing some insight from how Kubernetes handles selinux (which is, not well). One possibility to support Z and z flags in the kube yaml would be storing those flags in a kube annotation [similar to this](https://github.com/stardustapp/cloud-infra/blob/master/apps/linuxserver-base/statefulset.yaml#L17).

I am experiencing this same problem, but my system does not have SELinux, as I am using Ubuntu 20.04.

Should I open a new bug report?  If so I'll copy-paste, here's what I'm working with:

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST?**

Bug report

**Description**

On both systems I started with a clean slate when testing this procedure, neither had any pods or images when I started.

*On system "A"* I made a pod and a subsequent YAML file from the aforementioned pod with the help of instructions from this RedHat blog post:  https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/compose-podman-pods

The pod that was manually created works fine, it was a wp and mariadb container configured to produce a web page at port 8080.  I went to localhost:8080 and installed wp.

*On system "B"* I attempted to use the YAML file to create the pod on another similarly configured system (both running Ubuntu 20.04), but the pod created from the YAML did not work, attempting to connect to port 8080 (tested locally using curl and also tried from another system while firewall turned off) produced `error 403 forbidden`.

At this point *I removed the pods AND the images* from both systems and reversed steps on each one respectively - 

*On System "B"* I installed the pod starting from the very beginning with the same commands as previously used on system "A"

*On system "A"* I removed the pod and associated images and attempted to install using the YAML file (`podman play kube wptest.yaml`)

*System "B* which used commands for the pod worked fine (as tested using both curl locally and a remote connection), *System A* which used the YAML file produced error 403 `forbidden`.

Note:  I tried both the file created with `podman generate kube wptest > wptest.yaml` and a truncated copy in which libraries and other unnecessary criteria were removed, as described in the blog post  I referenced (I actually just copy-pasted it and replaced the names with `wptest`).   

**Describe the results you received:**

When using curl, browser, or remote system, (while firewall turned off) I received `error 403 forbidden`.

**Describe the results you expected:**

I expected the pod created by the YAML to behave the same as the pod created manually (allow connection, interaction with, and installation of the software present in the related containers).

**Output of podman version:**

```
Version:      2.0.4
API Version:  1
Go Version:   go1.14.2
Built:        Wed Dec 31 16:00:00 1969
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

All podman commands performed without `sudo` (e.g. rootless)

Is there any chance this could have to do with infra container interaction?

Is there any way I could modify the YAML so it would be more likely to act like the commands?
@averyfreeman can I see the kube yaml file you created?

> @averyfreeman can I see the kube yaml file you created?

Absolutely:

The YAML file auto-generated by `podman generate kube < wptestpod.yaml`:

```

# Save the output of this file and use kubectl create -f to import
# it into Kubernetes.
#
# Created with podman-2.0.4

metadata:
  creationTimestamp: '2020-08-26T17:02:56Z'
  labels:
    app: wptestpod
  name: wptestpod
spec:
  containers:
    - command:

The same YAML file with several removals referenced by https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/compose-podman-pods  :

```
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: '2020-08-26T17:02:56Z'
  labels:
    app: wptestpod
  name: wptestpod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: wptest-web
      env:
        - name: WORDPRESS_DB_NAME
          value: wp
        - name: WORDPRESS_DB_HOST
          value: 127.0.0.1
        - name: WORDPRESS_DB_USER
          value: wordpress
        - name: WORDPRESS_DB_PASSWORD
          value: testpassword
      image: docker.io/library/wordpress:latest
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80
          hostPort: 8080
          protocol: TCP
      resources: {}
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: true
        capabilities: {}
        privileged: false
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
        seLinuxOptions: {}
      workingDir: /var/www/html
    - name: wptest-db
      env:
        - name: MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD
          value: testpassword
        - name: MYSQL_USER
          value: wordpress
        - name: MYSQL_PASSWORD
          value: testpassword
        - name: MYSQL_DATABASE
          value: wp
      image: docker.io/library/mariadb:latest
      resources: {}
      securityContext:
        allowPrivilegeEscalation: true
        capabilities: {}
        privileged: false
        readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
        seLinuxOptions: {}
      workingDir: /
status: {}
```

Both produce same result (pod cannot be accessed, `403 error forbidden`) but were created from a working pod created by commands 

(again, I tested this twice on two similar systems, free of any pods or images at start, both for testing YAML and commands):

```
podman create --name wptestpod -p 8080:80

podman run \
-d --restart=always --pod=wptestpod2 \
-e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD="myrootpass" \
-e MYSQL_DATABASE="wp" \
-e MYSQL_USER="wordpress" \
-e MYSQL_PASSWORD="w0rdpr3ss" \
--name=wptest-db mariadb


podman run \
-d --restart=always --pod=wptestpod2 \
-e WORDPRESS_DB_NAME="wp" \
-e WORDPRESS_DB_USER="wordpress" \
-e WORDPRESS_DB_PASSWORD="w0rdpr3ss" \
-e WORDPRESS_DB_HOST="127.0.0.1" \
--name wptest-web wordpress
```  

Hope you can make use of this info. Thanks for your help!
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
This is still a problem for us, which complicates our workflow.

@ashley-cui has not been able to work on this, since she has been working mainly on podman secrets. 

@zhangguanzhang @Luap99 any others interested on working on a PR for this?

The second YAML provided from the article, works perfectly with latest 3.x code.
hmm, looks like this was fixed in one of previous releases. I currently on podman version 2.2.1 and all examples runs without any issues.
